textPlain: Thinking about it from the bee's perspective, this is like raiding the lair of an eldritch horror for gold. A beekeeper is just a funny looking bear-thing that takes honey sometimes, but the shop of a beekeeper is full of devices beyond a bee's comprehension, more honey than a bee would ever see in its lifetime just all sitting around, its own sun which can turn on and off. To find yourself in such a place by accident must be a crazy experience, convincing your brethren to attack it by shaking your butt is on another level.

In a bakery like 3 block away from my home, most days there are like 20 bees trying to steal the sweet cover over the pastries. But the front wall of the business is almost completely made of glass, so they can't escape.
Is it just me or would anyone else buy a video game based on this premise?It sorta reminds me of "Wax or the Discovery of Television Among the Bees."
From a bee's point of view humans are the robbers.
New word for me today: "apiarist"/"apiary". Never knew bee keepers had a more formal name, though it makes sense.Dad joke: It would be more apt if instead of a-piary, it was "b-piary".

Humans provide a sturdy, safe place to build hives and all they ask for in return is some of the excess honey. Bees make way more than they can use. Humans will also cart them around to food sources so they never have to worry about finding it. Seems like a sweet deal.
So a "swarm" is the collective noun for bees.  But I couldn't find a collective noun for apiarists.  I propose "stung" as in "a stung of beekeepers."
Bonus word: if you want to sound all fancy, beekeeping is also known as apiculture.
Jason Statham in "The Apiarist" doesn't have the same ring to it
